perturbation.original_text: 10. Confidentiality. Obligation of Confidentiality. Each party (the "Recipient") acknowledges that in connection with this Agreement such party may gain access to Confidential Information of the other party (the "Disclosing Party"). As a condition to being furnished with Confidential Information, the Recipient agrees, during the Term and for five (5) years thereafter. Confidential Information excludes such information required to be disclosed pursuant to federal or state securities rules and regulations, including but not limited to, disclosure of the Company in their filings with the Securities and Exchange Commission: to 10.1 (a) not use the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information other than in connection with performing its obligations under this Agreement and shall make no use of any such Confidential Information, directly or indirectly, in any manner to the detriment of the Disclosing Party or in order to obtain any competitive benefit with respect to the Disclosing Party; and (b) maintain the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information in confidence and, subject to Section 10.2 below, not disclose any of the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information without the Disclosing Party's prior written consent; provided, however, that Recipient may disclose the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information to its officers, employees, consultants and legal advisors ("Representatives") who: (i) have a "need to know" for purposes of the Recipient's performance under this Agreement, (ii) have been apprised of this restriction; and (iii) are themselves bound by nondisclosure restrictions at least as restrictive as those set forth in this Section 0.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. Confidentiality. Obligation of Confidentiality. Each party (the "Recipient") acknowledges that in connection with this Agreement such party may gain access to Confidential Information of the other party (the "Disclosing Party"). As a condition to being furnished with Confidential Information, the Recipient agrees, during the Term and for one (1) year thereafter. Confidential Information excludes such information required to be disclosed pursuant to federal or state securities rules and regulations, including but not limited to, disclosure of the Company in their filings with the Securities and Exchange Commission: to 10.1 (a) not use the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information other than in connection with performing its obligations under this Agreement and shall make no use of any such Confidential Information, directly or indirectly, in any manner to the detriment of the Disclosing Party or in order to obtain any competitive benefit with respect to the Disclosing Party; and (b) maintain the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information in confidence and, subject to Section 10.2 below, not disclose any of the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information without the Disclosing Party's prior written consent; provided, however, that Recipient may disclose the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information to its officers, employees, consultants and legal advisors ("Representatives") who: (i) have a "need to know" for purposes of the Recipient's performance under this Agreement, (ii) have been apprised of this restriction; and (iii) are themselves bound by nondisclosure restrictions at least as restrictive as those set forth in this Section 0.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year could be problematic because certain trade secrets, particularly those related to technology and business operations, can maintain their economic value for longer periods, and thus, under the UTSA, deserve longer protection. This change introduces a potential conflict with established trade secret law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Nevada law, specifically NRS 600A.030, defines misappropriation of trade secrets. By reducing the confidentiality period to one year, the agreement weakens the protection afforded to the Licensor's trade secrets. If information that qualifies as a trade secret under Nevada law is disclosed after the one-year period, it may be more difficult for the Licensor to prove misappropriation, especially if the information retains economic value beyond that year, potentially conflicting with the intent and scope of trade secret protection under Nevada law. The reasonableness of the duration of protection is a key factor in determining whether the information truly qualifies for trade secret protection and whether its misappropriation can be legally challenged.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NRS: CHAPTER 600A - TRADE SECRETS (UNIFORM ACT) [Rev. 6/29/2024 4:37:37 PM--2023] CHAPTER 600A - TRADE SECRETS (UNIFORM ACT) NRS 600A.010 Short title. NRS 600A.030 Definitions. NRS 600A.032 Presumption of reasonable effort by owner to maintain secrecy; rebuttal of presumption. NRS 600A.035 Theft of trade secrets prohibited; criminal penalties. NRS 600A.040 Actual or threatened misappropriation: Injunctive relief. NRS 600A.050 Misappropriation: Damages. NRS 600A.055 Misappropriation and posting or dissemination on Internet: Effect. NRS 600A.060 Attorneys fees. NRS 600A.070 Preservation of secrecy. NRS 600A.080 Limitation of actions. NRS 600A.090 Effect of chapter on other law and remedies. NRS 600A.100 Inapplicability of chapter to acts which occurred before, or began before and continue after, July 1, 1987. _________ NRS 600A.010 Short title. This chapter may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added to NRS by 1987, 19 ) NRS 600A.030 Definitions. As used in this chapter, unless the context otherwise requires: 1. Improper means includes, without limitation: (a) Theft; (b) Bribery; (c) Misrepresentation; (d) Willful breach or willful inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy; (e) Willful breach or willful inducement of a breach of a duty imposed by common law, statute, contract, license, protective order or other court or administrative order; and (f) Espionage through electronic or other means. 2. Misappropriation means: (a) Acquisition of the trade secret of another by a person by improper means; (b) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (c) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (1) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (2) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (I) Derived from or through a person who had used improper means to acquire it; (II) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (III)
perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the confidentiality period from five years to one year, potentially conflicting with trade secret law. The Law Explanation states that weakening the protection afforded to trade secrets can make it more difficult to prove misappropriation if the information retains economic value beyond that year. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'Misappropriation' in NRS 600A.030 as the acquisition, disclosure, or use of a trade secret without consent, especially if acquired through improper means or under circumstances imposing a duty to maintain secrecy. By reducing the confidentiality period, the agreement could be interpreted as no longer imposing a duty to maintain secrecy after one year, even if the information still qualifies as a trade secret, thus creating a legal contradiction because it undermines the protection afforded by trade secret law as defined in the statute.

perturbation.original_text: 6.1 Term. The initial term of this Agreement commences as of the Effective Date and, unless terminated earlier pursuant to any express provision of this Agreement, shall continue until five (5) years following the Effective Date (the "Initial Term"). Thereafter, this Agreement shall renew automatically for one (1) additional three (3) year period (the "Renewal Term" and collectively, together with the Initial Term, the "Term") unless either party provides the other with written notice of non-renewal at least ninety (90) days before the expiration of the Initial Term].
perturbation.changed_text: 6.1 Term. The initial term of this Agreement commences as of the Effective Date and, unless terminated earlier pursuant to any express provision of this Agreement, shall continue until five (5) years following the Effective Date (the "Initial Term"). Thereafter, this Agreement shall renew automatically for one (1) additional three (3) year period (the "Renewal Term" and collectively, together with the Initial Term, the "Term") unless either party provides the other with written notice of non-renewal at least thirty (30) days before the expiration of the Initial Term].
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the non-renewal notice period to 30 days may be insufficient under certain state laws that require longer notice periods for automatic renewal clauses, potentially making the renewal unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 22950 et seq.
perturbation.law_citation: California Business and Professions Code Section 22950
perturbation.law_explanation: Although the contract is governed by Nevada law, the Licensee has offices in Washington state, and could also be subject to California's automatic renewal laws if they solicit business in California or the end users access the content from California. California Business and Professions Code Section 22950 et seq. requires clear and conspicuous disclosure of automatic renewal terms and provides specific requirements for notice of renewal. While the exact notice period required depends on the nature of the service, significantly shortening the notice period to 30 days may not provide sufficient time for the customer to make an informed decision, potentially violating California's law, especially for contracts with longer initial terms.

This Division shall be known and may be referred to as the Stop Tobacco Access to Kids Enforcement Act or the STAKE Act. (Added by Stats. 1994, Ch. 1009, Sec. 1. Effective January 1, 1995.)
perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the non-renewal notice period from 90 to 30 days, which, according to the Law Explanation, may not provide sufficient time for an informed decision and could violate California's automatic renewal laws. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 focuses on tobacco access and doesn't directly address contract renewal notice periods, the Law Explanation suggests the contract could be subject to California Business and Professions Code Section 22950 et seq., which requires clear disclosure of automatic renewal terms and could be violated by the shortened notice period. The 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction' definition states that a contradiction exists when a contract sets a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable. This is precisely what the changed text does by potentially violating California law with the reduced notice period.
